request.question: What award did the creator of the HTO receive in the WW?

title: Hermann Göring
Katsuhiro Otomo
Howard E. Greer
President's Volunteer Service Award
Charles Upham
Amory Adventure Award
Golden Globe Cecil B. DeMille Award
Commissioner's Historic Achievement Award
San Francisco World Spirits Competition
Haupttreuhandstelle Ost
sentences: Hermann Wilhelm Göring (or Goering; ] ; 12 January 1893 – 15 October 1946) was a German political and military leader as well as one of the most powerful figures in the Nazi Party (NSDAP) that ruled Germany from 1933 to 1945.
A veteran World War I fighter pilot ace, he was a recipient of the "Pour le Mérite".
He was the last commander of "Jagdgeschwader" 1, the fighter wing once led by Manfred von Richthofen.
Katsuhiro Otomo (大友 克洋 , Ōtomo Katsuhiro , born April 14, 1954) is a Japanese manga artist, screenwriter and film director.
He is best known as the creator of the manga "Akira" and its animated film adaptation.

He was decorated a "Chevalier" of the French Ordre des Arts et des Lettres in 2005, promoted to "Officier" of the order in 2014, became the fourth manga artist ever inducted into the American Eisner Award Hall of Fame in 2012, and was awarded the Purple Medal of Honor from the Japanese government in 2013.
Otomo later received the Winsor McCay Award at the 41st Annie Awards in 2014 and the 2015 Grand Prix de la ville d'Angoulême, the first manga artist to receive the award.
Howard Earl Greer (May 1, 1921 – November 22, 2015) was a vice admiral in the United States Navy.
He is a former commander of the USS "Hancock" (CV-19), chief of staff of Naval Air Force Pacific, commander Naval Air Reserve Force, and Commander, Naval Air Force U.S. Atlantic Fleet.
His awards include 2 Distinguished Service Medals, 4 Legions of Merit, the Navy Commendation Medal, Pacific Theater WW II Ribbon with 9 Battle Stars and the Vietnamese Distinguished Service Award.
He died in 2015.
The President's Volunteer Service Award is a civil award bestowed by the President of the United States.

Established by executive order by George W. Bush, the award was established to honor volunteers that give hundreds of hours per year helping others through the President's Council on Service and Civic Participation.
The award can be granted to individuals, families and organizations located throughout the United States.

Depending on the amount of service hours completed, individuals can receive the Bronze, Silver, Gold, and/or the President's Call to Service Award (also referred to the President's Lifetime Achievement Award).

The Call to Service Award is the most prestigious, and it has been awarded to few Americans to recognize over 4,000 hours of extraordinary service including honorees such as S. Truett Cathy, Mark Carman, Zach Bonner, Brandon Pugh, Thomas Smith, and Michael Taggart.
Awardees may receive a personalized certificate, an official pin, medallion, and/or a congratulatory letter from the President depending on the award earned.

Charles Hazlitt Upham, (21 September 1908 – 22 November 1994) was a New Zealand soldier who earned the Victoria Cross (VC) twice during the Second World War; in Crete in May 1941, and at Ruweisat Ridge, Egypt, in July 1942.
He was the most recent of only three people to receive the VC twice, the only one to receive two VCs during the Second World War and the only combat soldier to receive the award twice.
As a result, Upham is often described as the most highly decorated Commonwealth soldier of that war, as the VC is the Commonwealth's highest award for gallantry in the face of the enemy.
The Amory Adventure Award is an award in the Canadian Venturer program.
Unlike all other awards and badges, for example the Queen's Venturer Award, the Amory Award is only granted to one Venturer company each year.
All Venturers who take part in an Amory Award expedition (whether or not they are members of the winning company) receive a participation badge to wear on the uniform.
The first-place company's name is recorded on the Amory Adventure Trophy, which the company may keep for nine months.
Companies that place first, second, and third each receive a trophy of their own to keep.
The Cecil B. DeMille Award is an honorary Golden Globe Award bestowed by the Hollywood Foreign Press Association for "outstanding contributions to the world of entertainment".
It was first presented on February 21, 1952 at the 9th Annual Golden Globe Awards ceremony and is named in honor of its first recipient, director Cecil B. DeMille.
Honorees are selected by the HFPA board of directors and are presented annually (except for 1976 and 2008).

The first woman to receive the honor was Judy Garland in 1962 who, at 39 years of age, was also the youngest honoree ever to receive the award, while Samuel Goldwyn, at the age of 93, in 1973, was the oldest.
The first African-American to receive the honor was Sidney Poitier in 1982.
The Commissioner's Historic Achievement Award is awarded by the Commissioner of Baseball to a group or person who has made a "major impact on the sport" of baseball.
It is not an annual award; rather, the Commissioner presents the trophy at his discretion.

The trophy is a gold baseball sitting atop a cylindrical silver base, created by Tiffany & Co. The award has been presented fourteen times by Commissioner Bud Selig: eleven times to players, once to a team, and twice to a non-player.
Mark McGwire and Sammy Sosa were the first to receive the award for their parts in the 1998 MLB home run record chase.
The most recent recipient is Derek Jeter (2014), who was honored in the final year of his career for being "one of the most accomplished shortstops of all-time".
The 2001 Seattle Mariners won the award as a team for posting a 116–46 record one season after losing Alex Rodriguez to the Texas Rangers.
Roberto Clemente, the 2006 awardee, is the only player to receive the award posthumously; his award was accepted by his wife, Vera.
The San Francisco World Spirits Competition was founded in 2000 by Anthony Dias Blue.
Blue is the current director of both the San Francisco World Spirits Competition and the San Francisco International Wine Competition.

It assesses hundreds of entrants annually (1,407 in 2013 from 63 countries) with tastings involving panels of expert judges selected each year from the spirits industry including world-renowned mixologists, spirits buyers, and media from across the United States.
Producers must submit their product for the competition and pay a fee ($475 for 2013) for its evaluation.
Not all entries are given awards (those not judged of sufficient quality are not given an award) but most receive a bronze, silver, or gold award from the tasting panel.

The fact that most entrants receive an award likely involves some degree of self-selection, as the spirits producers choose whether to enter each of their brands in the competition and pay to receive a rating.
Those entrants that are given a unanimous gold medal by the panel are given the distinction of a "double-gold" medal.
Additionally, a "best in show" designation is awarded in each main category of spirits.

The Haupttreuhandstelle Ost (HTO), or the Main Trustee Office for the East, created by Hermann Göring, was a Nazi German predatory state institution responsible for liquidating Polish and Jewish businesses in occupied Poland or selling them to German "settlers" from the East for a symbolic fee during World War II.
It was staffed by "a veritable stampede" of officials from the Deutsche and Dresdner banks.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines) in this exact order:

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target, the passage id(s) that mention it in square brackets, and one preserved verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. If multiple passages contain the same exact quoted excerpt for the same target, collapse them into a single line listing that target — [comma-separated passage ids] "verbatim excerpt". Preserve quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages.

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — For each named entity or relation needed to answer the question that is absent or unresolved in the passages, state concisely the exact missing relation (e.g., "Need <Entity>'s founding year", "Need author of adaptation", "Need award count for <Entity>"). If the question names multiple entities and any named entity is missing from the passages, add one Bridge line per missing entity describing the exact missing relation. If all required relations and entity disambiguations are explicitly present and unambiguous in the passages, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion on a single line here.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next hops the next agent should run: exact page titles (e.g., "<Entity>") or quoted search queries (e.g., "<Entity> awards"). When relevant, append specific page sections to check in parentheses (lead, Infobox, "Awards"/"Honours"/"Recognition", "History"/"Publication history"). Prioritize the most likely targets first. End this section with the standardized deferral sentence exactly as written: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below." If the passages already supply all required facts unambiguously, you may omit retrieval targets and instead state that no further retrieval is needed.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above, in that order, and use the exact section labels shown.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts ≤15 words, and numeric/date tokens) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Collapse duplicate identical evidence per target as specified above to keep Evidence found concise.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (that is: each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and every needed relation value is explicitly present). If any required fact or disambiguation is missing or ambiguous, explicitly defer using the standardized deferral sentence in Retrieval direction.
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Agent role: Produce one compact second-hop retrieval query targeting the missing bridge or supporting evidence.

Pipeline position: after summary1 and before second-hop retrieval. Inputs are the original question and the first-hop intermediate state. Functional responsibility: produce one focused retrieval query that is likely to recover the missing evidence needed by the downstream agents. Query quality is determined by evidence recovery, not by whether the query itself resembles or contains the final answer.

Native output contract: The module must consume `question` and `summary_1` and produce exactly one compact second-hop query field named `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- Prefer an explicit page-title token sequence: if `summary_1` contains an explicit page-title token sequence (for example an ExactPageTitle or an instruction-like Fetch page title), set the single `query` to that exact token sequence verbatim (aside from light typo fixes). This exact page-title takes precedence over composing a different token list.
- When the inputs mention two or more entities or titles that must be linked, include every distinct entity token visible in the inputs and include any adjacent disambiguator tokens or explicit retrieval cues (multiword service names, years, edition names, "release date", "regular season"). Place at most one neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the targeted relation.
- If `summary_1` already states the answer-bearing attribute (service, network, type token such as "band", "film", or a specific service name), omit that asserted attribute from the query and instead focus the query on the bridge relation by using the entity tokens and disambiguators that point toward the supporting page (for example creator+series, member+group, author+radio talks).
- When the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond token-level fixes.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and at most one allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise plain-text summary string (no extra fields, labels, markup, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification.
- Primary goal: explicitly integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the supported linkage it needs.

Explicit bridging requirement:
- When the passages supply a clear linking fact between the context entity and information in the passages, produce a concise 1–2 sentence integrated summary that: (1) names the context entity using the exact form present in `context`, (2) names the supporting entity or attribute using the exact form present in `passages`, and (3) states the explicit relation (membership, creator attribution, parentage, partnership, award, representation, founding, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Phrase this as one short bridging sentence when possible.
- When a passage that supplies the linking fact also presents a canonical name variant or a parenthetical birthdate (for example, a parenthetical beginning with 'born' or an alternate spelling), append that exact parenthetical or variant immediately after the corresponding canonical name in the summary — but only when (a) the passage with the disambiguator is the same passage that supports the linking relation, and (b) including the disambiguator is necessary to resolve identity; if multiple passages give conflicting disambiguators, omit them.
- For relations that naturally involve two distinct facts (for example, parentage plus representation, or a person linked to a show and also to an occupation), it is acceptable to use two short sentences within the same single-string summary: e.g., one sentence for parentage and one for representation. Still observe the 1–2 sentence limit.

Inclusion of passage-supplied disambiguators (only when directly relevant):
- If a passage supplies canonical multi-token names or other disambiguating attributes needed to resolve the question, include them inline using the wording from the passages: append birthdates in parentheses immediately after the associated full canonical name (e.g., Name (YYYY–) ), state group type and formation year using the passage's phrasing (e.g., "a [group type] formed in [year]"), and include a canonical member list exactly as shown (comma-separated) only when the passage presents a definitive membership list. Do not add ancillary attributes that are not necessary to the identification or explicit linking required by the question.
- Only include such disambiguators when the passage that supplies the linking fact also contains them and when they are needed to disambiguate; do not append passage parentheticals or variants automatically in other cases.

Multiple candidates and ambiguity:
- If passages present multiple candidate referents for a question's ambiguous target, then: (a) if one candidate is directly linked to the `context` by an explicit passage-supported relation, assert that bridge concisely; (b) otherwise, produce at most two short sentences: one naming the `context` and linking it as far as supported, and a second that lists the candidate names exactly as shown in `passages` with only the narrowly relevant attributes (e.g., records, occupation) and a concise statement that the intended referent is unclear. Use passage wording for the attributes.

Comparisons, membership-plus-occupation linkages, and structuring facts:
- When the linking relation naturally pairs membership/representation with occupation, nationality, or similar attributes, separate these into distinct concise clauses or sentences: one clause/sentence should state the membership or linkage using the exact context name and passage wording, and a second clause/sentence should state the occupation/nationality or other attribute phrase for the same entity using passage wording.
- For direct comparisons between two named subjects, emit one concise passage-grounded sentence per named subject (at most two short sentences total) and explicitly note when the passages do not supply a comparative metric.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (e.g., "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages, comma-separated if multiple. Do not add explanatory clauses, relations, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (for example: "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Preserve uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 short sentences). Do not expand into biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Use only facts and phrasing present in `context` and `passages`; do not introduce or infer unsupported information.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.
- Do not assert that a passage's member list is complete unless the passage explicitly states it is definitive.

Format enforcement:
- Return a single plain-text string only. Do not emit JSON, dictionaries, labels, bullets, or any structured artifact—only the concise natural-language summary string.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else. Before emitting that string, you may apply only the narrowly permitted normalizations and explicit extraction rules listed below. After applying permitted normalizations/extraction, the emitted string must equal the minimal label text from the prioritized summary verbatim (see normalization and extraction constraints). Specific allowed single-token answers: for a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no. For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces), subject only to the permitted normalizations and extraction rules. Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if a permitted normalization or explicit extraction rule applies (see below).

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) and the permitted normalizations/extraction rules as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Permitted normalizations and explicit extraction rules (apply only when the token(s) to emit appear verbatim as a contiguous substring in the prioritized summary):
- General principle: prefer and extract the smallest contiguous substring from the prioritized summary (summary_2, or summary_1 only if summary_2 is silent for the answer) that directly corresponds verbatim to the question's requested component (for example: city from a "City, Region" token; country when comparing locations; a middle name inside a full personal name; a core proper name embedded in a longer phrase). Emit that contiguous substring exactly as it appears after trimming surrounding punctuation/whitespace.
- Allowed wrapper removals (only when the target substring appears exactly as a contiguous sequence in the prioritized summary):
  - Comma-separated place forms: when a place is presented as "City, Region", and the question requests the city/hometown, emit the contiguous substring before the first comma (the city) exactly as shown.
  - Simple prepositional wrappers: when a place-name appears inside a short prepositional phrase such as "in <Place>", "located in <Place>", or "from <Place>", emit the contiguous place-name noun phrase verbatim (do not emit the preposition).
  - Leading definite-article + 'of' band/organization patterns: when a name appears in patterns like "The <Core> of <Place>", and the question requests the core name, emit the contiguous core proper-name substring exactly as shown (for example extract "<Core>").
  - Trailing corporate suffixes or parenthetical acronyms: if the prioritized summary contains a company name with explicit trailing tokens like "Inc.", "LLC", ".Ltd", or an immediately adjacent parenthetical acronym (e.g., "Example Corp. (EX)"), you may strip those trailing corporate suffixes/parentheticals and emit the core company name contiguous substring exactly as shown.
  - Middle-name extraction: when the prioritized summary presents a full personal name and the question requests a middle name, emit the contiguous middle name token(s) from that full name verbatim.
  - Trailing single generic descriptor removal: if the prioritized summary presents a proper name followed immediately by a single generic descriptor token in the form "<ProperName> <generic>" (for example "Name crisis"), and the question's scope is the proper name, emit only the proper-name contiguous substring exactly as shown.
- Existing permitted normalizations retained and scoped to the contiguous-substring rule: parenthetical/core-name stripping, hyphenated composite phrase extraction, trailing generic-descriptor removal, and denomination-occupation reduction are allowed only when the exact form to be normalized appears verbatim as a contiguous substring in the prioritized summary.
- Normalization/extraction constraints:
  - Do not apply any normalization or extraction unless the text to emit appears verbatim as a contiguous substring in the prioritized summary (summary_2 prioritized, or summary_1 only if summary_2 is silent for the answer).
  - Normalizations and extractions may only remove or trim tokens; they must not add, invent, re-order, or substitute new content.
  - Maintain original capitalization and word order of the retained substring, except for trimming surrounding punctuation/whitespace as allowed above.
- Explicit prohibitions: always forbid adding or appending any non-verbatim qualifiers or wrapper tokens. Do not append commas, locations, parentheses, role descriptors, explanatory prefixes/suffixes, or geographic/species qualifiers that are not part of the exact contiguous substring you are emitting from the prioritized summary.

Tie-break and variant-selection rules:
- If a role-descriptor and a proper name co-occur in the prioritized summary in the form "<role> <Name>", prefer emitting the named individual exactly as shown (emit the Name only) unless the prioritized summary presents the role as the requested concise label explicitly without a name.
- For title or label-variant conflicts, prefer an alternate or parenthetical variant only when the prioritized summary explicitly marks that variant as an alias, alternate name, or the original-language title (or otherwise explicitly labels it as a variant) AND that variant matches the question's scope. If no such explicit labeling appears, emit the primary label supported by summary_2.

Strict emission rule (verbatim-emission):
- The final `answer` must equal the minimal label text from the prioritized summary verbatim after applying only the permitted normalizations/extraction rules above. Do not append or prepend occupational nouns, role descriptors, location qualifiers, or explanatory tokens that are not part of that verbatim label.

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

Additionally, a "best in show" designation is awarded in each main category of spirits.

The Haupttreuhandstelle Ost (HTO), or the Main Trustee Office for the East, created by Hermann Göring, was a Nazi German predatory state institution responsible for liquidating Polish and Jewish businesses in occupied Poland or selling them to German "settlers" from the East for a symbolic fee during World War II.
It was staffed by "a veritable stampede" of officials from the Deutsche and Dresdner banks.

response.hop1_query: What award did the creator of the HTO receive in the WW?
hop1_docs: I Saw What You Did (1988 film) | I Saw What You Did is a 1988 American made-for-television horror film directed by Fred Walton.  It is a remake of the 1965 theatrical film of the same name starring Joan Crawford.  It received generally negative reviews, with only a few exceptions.  Nevertheless, it won an Emmy Award in the category "Outstanding Cinematography for a Miniseries or a Special".

Tax wedge | The tax wedge is the deviation from the equilibrium price/quantity (formula_1 and formula_2, respectively) as a result of the taxation of a good.  Because of the tax, consumers pay more for the good (formula_3) than they did before the tax, and suppliers receive less for the good (formula_4) than they did before the tax .  Put differently, the tax wedge is the difference between what consumers pay and what producers receive (net of tax) from a transaction.  The tax effectively drives a "wedge" between the price consumers pay and the price producers receive for a product.
HTO Park | HTO Park (stylized as HO) is an urban beach in Toronto, Ontario, Canada, that opened in 2007.  It is located west of Harbourfront Centre, on Lake Ontario.

Kenny Hotz | Kenneth Joel "Kenny" Hotz is a Canadian comedy writer, producer, director, actor and comedian.  Hotz is a "South Park" consultant and writer, creator/star of the Comedy Central television show "Kenny vs. Spenny", creator and writer of the FX series "Testees", and the creator/star of "Kenny Hotz's Triumph of the Will".  He has received numerous awards for his television work— from the Academy of Canadian Cinema & Television the Gemini Awards and the Canadian Comedy Awards—and is a multiple-time film festival award-winner.  He is also an award-winning "Vice" contributor and Gulf war photo-journalist.

Panel Syndicate | Panel Syndicate is an online publisher of DRM-free pay what you want webcomics in multiple languages, founded by Marcos Martín to publish his and Brian K. Vaughan's creator-owned comic "The Private Eye" in March 2013.  To date Panel Syndicate has published comics in English, Spanish, Catalan, Portuguese, and French, with additional languages in development.  "The Private Eye" has received critical acclaim and media attention for its role as one of the first DRM-free, pay what you want comics by creators of Martín and Vaughan's caliber.  Initially an outlet for publishing Vaughan and Martín's collaborations, Panel Syndicate is open to publishing other creators' works by using the same DRM-free, pay what you want model.

What Richard Did | What Richard Did is a 2012 Irish film directed by Lenny Abrahamson and written by Malcolm Campbell.  The film is loosely based on Kevin Power's "Bad Day in Blackrock", a fictionalised novel inspired by the real-life death of Brian Murphy in 2000.  It won the best Irish film of the year award at the 10th Irish Film & Television Awards and was the most commercially successful Irish film of 2012.

Lena Dunham | Lena Dunham ( ; born May 13, 1986) is an American actress, writer, producer, and director.  She is best known as the creator, writer and star of the HBO series "Girls" (2012–2017), for which she has received numerous Emmy Award nominations and two Golden Globe Awards. Dunham's work on "Girls" also led her to become the first woman to win the Directors Guild of America Award for Outstanding Directing – Comedy Series in 2013.  That year Dunham was included in the "Time" 100 list of the most influential people in the world.  In 2014, Dunham released her first book, "Not That Kind of Girl: A Young Woman Tells You What She's "Learned"".

hop2_docs: HTO Park | HTO Park (stylized as HO) is an urban beach in Toronto, Ontario, Canada, that opened in 2007.  It is located west of Harbourfront Centre, on Lake Ontario.

Sugar Beach | Sugar Beach is an urban beach park in Toronto, Ontario, Canada, that opened in 2010.  It is located across from Redpath Sugar Refinery in Toronto's eastern East Bayfront.  Like HTO Park to the west, the beach is not meant to allow wading or swimming in Lake Ontario, but rather functions as a waterfront public space for relaxation, leisure, and social activities.  It cost $14 million.

East Hampton Airport | East Hampton Airport (IATA: HTO, ICAO: KHTO, FAA LID: HTO) is a public use airport located three nautical miles (6 km) west of East Hampton, a village in the Town of East Hampton, Suffolk County, New York, United States.  The airport is owned and operated by the Town of East Hampton and located in the town's hamlet of Wainscott.  It is included in the National Plan of Integrated Airport Systems for 2011–2015, which categorized it as a "general aviation" facility.

Hey Mister (I Need This Job) | "Hey Mister (I Need This Job)" is a song recorded by American country music group Shenandoah.  It was released in August 1992 as the second single from the album "Long Time Comin'".  The song reached #28 on the "Billboard" Hto Country Singles & Tracks chart.  The song was written by Kerry Chater and Renee Armand.

Haupttreuhandstelle Ost | The Haupttreuhandstelle Ost (HTO), or the Main Trustee Office for the East, created by Hermann Göring, was a Nazi German predatory state institution responsible for liquidating Polish and Jewish businesses in occupied Poland or selling them to German "settlers" from the East for a symbolic fee during World War II.  It was staffed by "a veritable stampede" of officials from the Deutsche and Dresdner banks.

Grand Canyon Park Operations Building | Grand Canyon Park Operations Building, was built in 1929 on the South Rim of the Grand Canyon in Grand Canyon National Park.  It is significant as an example of a National Park Service building designed to blend harmoniously with the natural surroundings, in the National Park Service Rustic style.  The Operations Building was designed to replace the Superintendent's Residence as the park headquarters.  It was in turn replaced by a newer building in 1967, and presently functions as the headquarters for park law enforcement.  The building was designed by the National Park Service Landscape Division under the direction of Thomas Chalmers Vint, and has been designated a National Historic Landmark for its design significance.

Shawnee Park | Shawnee Park is a municipal park in Louisville, Kentucky.  It was designed by Frederick Law Olmsted, who designed 18 of the city's 123 public parks.  Along with the rest of the city's Olmsted-designed park system, Shawnee Park was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1982.
